title: Nested containers has wrong permissions when using `--userns:keep-id`
body:
### Issue Description Discovering the possibility to nest containers in https://github.com/gabyx/container-nesting (which was specifically made for this bug report to properly test this). This repo uses the podman image to nest containers, so doing podman-in-podman-in-podman-..... *Note: This container nesting is not some academic example (although for nesting levels >2 it migth be), this usecase appears in CI when we want the ability to not have everything in one top-level image only but leave room to run other containers inside the top-level one.* Container nesting works with user `root`. It seems that when running the container nesting recursion as user `podman` together with `--userns=keep-id:uid=1000,gid=1000` something is at odd when looking at the permission how the files are mounted at nesting level >1 (here only `/home/podman` is relevant but other directories have similar issues). I dont think its a podman issue (hopefully) but maybe a image issue or a configuration issue. It would however be nice if the podman image would support such kind of nesting (1-2 levels). ### Steps to reproduce the issue Steps to reproduce the issue 1. `git clone --branch bugfix/report-bug-podman https://github.com/gabyx/container-nesting` 2. `cd container-nesting` 3. Check that the root recursion works: ```shell just podman custom root ``` or ```shell cd src/podman && start-run.sh custom root false ``` The output should be 
     ===========================================================
     ===========================================================
     ==================== Start Recursion ======================
     ===========================================================
     ===========================================================
    | -> 1. Container: inside [version: 36ff37d9a63c55f5974889fcc8b778c4]
    | -> 1. Container: Launching a new container ...
    | -> 1. Container: create volume:
    | podman-root-1
    | -> 1. Container: Simple container run test:
    | NAME="Alpine Linux"
    | -> 1. Container: Start new container:
    |
    | | -> 2. Container: inside [version: 36ff37d9a63c55f5974889fcc8b778c4]
    | | -> 2. Container: Launching a new container ...
    | | -> 2. Container: create volume:
    | | podman-root-2
    | | -> 2. Container: Simple container run test:
    | | NAME="Alpine Linux"
    | | -> 2. Container: Start new container:
    | |
    | | | -> 3. Container: inside [version: 36ff37d9a63c55f5974889fcc8b778c4]
    | | | -> 3. Container: Launching a new container ...
    | | | -> 3. Container: create volume:
    | | | podman-root-3
    | | | -> 3. Container: Simple container run test:
    | | | NAME="Alpine Linux"
    | | | -> 3. Container: Start new container:
    | | |
    | | | | -> 4. Container: inside [version: 36ff37d9a63c55f5974889fcc8b778c4]
    | | | | -> 4. Container: Launching a new container ...
    | | | | -> 4. Container: create volume:
    | | | | podman-root-4
    | | | | -> 4. Container: Simple container run test:
    | | | | NAME="Alpine Linux"
    | | | | -> 4. Container: Start new container:
    | | | |
    | | | | | -> 5. Container: inside [version: 36ff37d9a63c55f5974889fcc8b778c4]
    | | | | | -> 5. Container: Finally reached container level: 5
    | | | | | -> 5. Container: leaving
    | | | |
    | | | | -> 4. Container: leaving
    | | |
    | | | -> 3. Container: leaving
    | |
    | | -> 2. Container: leaving
    |
    | -> 1. Container: leaving
1. Run the recursion with user `podman`. This will use `--userns=keepid:uid=1000,gid=1000` to make all mounts use `podman` user. ```shell cd src/podman && start-run.sh custom podman false ``` This will crash. To inspect whats going on use `start-run.sh custom podman true` which will put you in a `bash` shell right after entering every container and right after leaving. Exit the shell to continue the recursion. 2. Inspect inside the `-> 1. Container: entering` that `ls -alnd /home/podman` has permission `1000`. 2. Inspect inside the `-> 2. Container: entering` that `ls -aln /home/podman` has **not permission `1000`** but on my machine its `999` which is weird. **Note:** The image which is used is an `alpine` image with podman installed. You can also use `original` instead of `custom` above to build and use the original `quay.io/podman/stable` image. The findings are the same. ### Describe the results you received The permissions on `ls -aln /home/podman` result in 1000 in the 1. container and **not** 1000 in the 2. container. when `podman run --userns=keepid:uid=1000,gid=1000 ...` is launched from within the 1. container. ### Describe the results you expected The `/home/podman` directory should be mounted with permissions `1000` also in the 2. container and on further deeper levels. **Note**: I am using `--privileged` all the time, not sure if thats wrong... ### podman info output

  cpuUtilization:

    - container_id: 0

    - container_id: 0

    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 100000

  kernel: 6.6.19

comments.body:
> The /home/podman directory should be mounted with permissions 1000 also in the 2. container and on further deeper levels.

User-namespaces can also be nested, and it get's really super confusing.  But for the 2. continaer, check the podman entry in 1. container's /etc/sub{u,g}id files.  There is an expected second translation based on the entries.

I think perhaps what you're after in the 2. container, is to run the nested podman with additional `--userns=keep-id:...` options to setup the maps how you expect.  I expect this will become confusing very quickly (it already hurts my brain).

@cevich : It hurts your brain because you understand the topic, haha, I dont so I am basically on painkillers =).

So as my understanding goes: The  1. container's `/etc/subuid` just contains `podman:1:999` and `podman:1001:...` 
and when `podman` runs in `container1` these definitions are in effect when doing another `podman run ...` inside `container1`. 

And a `podman run` in `container1` will do `newuidmap`, so are these lines in `/etc/subuid` always with respect to the host or to the current container? (jeah its confusing, and without some expert showing an explanation whats needed at which level, I cannot phrase it better)

how is this different than https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/22049 ?

Is there anything to fix in Podman?  Otherwise, if it is not clear there is a Podman issue, let's close this issue and keep the discussion in one place.

The issue here is basically to point towards a strange error, possible in the implementation of podman's `keep-id` implementation (but I can only guess) or the fact that the podman image is not capable of running another container inside it (nesting level 2):

When I start a container at level 1 (inside first container) with
-  `podman run -it --userns=keep-id -d ttl.sh/podman-test tail -f /dev/null`  
    (use `just run custom podman true` and in the first shell == 1. container)
- then find the `pid` of `tail` with `ps`
- `cat /proc/$pid_of_tail/uid_map`:
  ```
  d2f2f4f2945c:~$ cat /proc/$pid_of_tail/uid_map
         0          1        999
       999       1001          1
      1000       1000          1
      1001       1002      64534
  ```

  This map maps `uid` in namespace of `tail` (container 2) to `uid`s in the first container. (see explanation of [uid maps](https://ifeanyi.co/posts/linux-namespaces-part-2])
  It shows the following mapping:

  | in container 2 | ---maps to--->| in container 1|
  | --- |--- | --- |
  |`[root=0 .... 998]` |  | `[1...999]` |
  | `999` | | `1001` |
  | `1000` (podman)| | `1000` (podman) |
  | `[1001...1001+64533]` |  | `[1002...1002+64533]` |

- **When you print `ls -alnd /home/podman` in the second container you find id `999` which is utterly strange, because:
  the `/home/podman` in the image `ttl.sh/podman-test` has id `1000`.???**:
  ```shell
  ls -alnd /home/podman
  drwxr-sr-x    4 999      999             12 Mar 19 14:35 /home/podman
  ```

do you get the correct ownership if you don't use any shared store?

There is no guarantee that a shared store will have the correct ownership for its files, unless you use fuse-overlayfs in a way that it does mapping of IDs at runtime, and the files in the store you want to share have the correct ownership in a xattr (using `force_mask`): https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6206192

> unless you use fuse-overlayfs

IIUC, the overlay-in-overlay restriction also still applies.

> The issue here is basically to point towards a strange error

I like issues for errors. However in this case I think the error is expected, though trying to reason out the mappings will give anybody a headache.

If giuseppe agrees, my preference would also be to close this and continue forward in the https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/22049 discussion.  Though I don't fault you for opening the issue, this stuff is really really complicated.

@giuseppe : Whats a shared store? Podman on my NixOS has `graphdrivername: overlay` and in the image its also `overlay` but also there is `overlay.mount_program` which points to `fuse-overlayfs` so its not OverlayFS (overlay2 (?).
How can I test this?

We can continue in the discussion, I can copy over the stuff there, for better reference...

**update**: Ah I think you wanted me to delete the line : https://github.com/gabyx/container-nesting/blob/main/src/podman/container/run.sh#L81
which shares the store with the next container.

@giuseppe : You are right:
When I remove 
- https://github.com/gabyx/container-nesting/blob/main/src/podman/container/run.sh#L81
- https://github.com/gabyx/container-nesting/blob/main/src/podman/start-run.sh#L40
I get the same uid map run from 1 container for `tail` in the second container :

```shell
cat /proc/468/uid_map
         0          1        999
       999       1001          1
      1000       1000          1
      1001       1002      64534
```

**But the permissions on `/home/podman` are now correct to `1000`.**

> There is no guarantee that a shared store will have the correct ownership for its files
I cannot access your solution to this link, would you share the gist of it?

When I run the recursion with `just podman custom podman` I get to container level 2 where it fails to start a 3rd container with:

```
-> 2. Container: inside [version: 9717e127c2446a0c85d1eb95d7ca0123]
Bash when entering container:

e0a7410ef2e4:~$ podman run -it --userns=keep-id -d ttl.sh/podman-test tail -f /dev/null

ERRO[0000] running `/usr/bin/newuidmap 20 0 1000 1 1 1 999 1000 1001 64535`: Could not open proc directory for target 20: No such file or directory
usage: newuidmap [<pid>|fd:<pidfd>] <uid> <loweruid> <count> [ <uid> <loweruid> <count> ] ...
ERRO[0000] Failed to check for setuid on /usr/bin/newuidmap: invalid argument
Error: cannot set up namespace using "/usr/bin/newuidmap": exit status 1
```

> @giuseppe : Whats a shared store? Podman on my NixOS has `graphdrivername: overlay` and in the image its also `overlay` but also there is `overlay.mount_program` which points to `fuse-overlayfs` so its not OverlayFS (overlay2 (?).
> How can I test this?

the store you share with the nested containers must have been created with `fuse-overlays` as mount_program and it must have the following configuration:

```
[storage.options.overlay]
force_mask = "shared"
```

after you pull the images, also make sure all the files in the store are world readable, so you need:

```
chmod -R 755 /path/to/the/store
```

In the nested containers then you need to use fuse-overlayfs.

Since it is not a podman issue we need to address.  I am closing this issue now
